summary: I was born in Yamabuki, Shimoina-Gun, Nagano-Ken, Japan in 1890. My father was the principal of the Yamabuki grammar school there, and although he valued education highly, our family was poor and I was denied the chance to pursue schooling during my early years. Life in Yamabuki was difficult, especially after my mother passed away when I was two, and I was raised by my maternal grandmother.

Japan was my homeland where I spent my childhood and early adulthood. After my mother died, my family was separated, and I eventually moved to Nara with my stepmother and brother. My father left Japan to study in America, and later I returned to Japan briefly before moving to the United States in 1915. The customs and traditions of Japan, including arranged marriage, shaped much of my early life.

When I was about 12 years old, I moved to Nara to live with my stepmother and brother. It was my first time traveling by train and I was awed by the city compared to my mountain village. Although the promise of education was not immediate, I later attended Giei Gakko in nearby Gose and graduated with honors after four years of study.

I came to the United States in September 1915 to join my husband, who had returned from his studies in America. Upon arrival, I was welcomed warmly and introduced to a church community where I was baptized. My life here was filled with both joy and hardship, including personal struggles and later the challenges of wartime internment. Despite difficulties, the U.S. became my home for many decades.

Yohei Tanaka was a young man who had just been baptized when he kindly offered to take me to church after I arrived in the United States. From that day until his death in 1973, his family acted as constant guardians for mine. He played an important role in helping me adjust to my new faith and life in America.

Rev. Kiyama was a minister from Japan who gave a sermon titled 'The Joy of Belief in Jesus Christ' that profoundly changed my life. I met him during a very difficult time after my separation from my husband, and his message based on John 3:16 helped me find true faith and redemption. That Sunday marked a spiritual rebirth for me and set the course for my future.

The Bible was central to my spiritual journey, especially after I moved to the United States. Although I initially struggled to understand its meaning, with help from church members like Mr. Gammier and Rev. Kiyama, I was able to grasp its teachings. It became a source of comfort and guidance throughout my life, particularly during times of hardship.

John 3:16 was the scripture that Rev. Kiyama used in his sermon which deeply moved me. The verse about God's love and salvation helped me realize the shallowness of my previous faith and inspired me to seek true redemption. It remains a pivotal scripture in my spiritual life.

Mr. Gammier was a renowned French designer who took me on as an apprentice when I decided to pursue sewing and design to support myself. Under his expert guidance, I refined my skills over two years and was encouraged by him to open my own shop. However, due to racial tensions in Southern California, I chose instead to work for Madame Alma, with Mr. Gammier's understanding and support.

Southern California was where Mr. Gammier accepted a position in the motion picture industry, leading to the closure of his business. It was also a place where I considered opening my own shop, but due to strong racial antagonism toward Japanese residents, I decided against it. This environment influenced many of my career decisions during that time.

Madame Alma was a demanding yet warm and hardworking employer who hired me as a table manager after my apprenticeship. Despite warnings about her difficult nature, I found our working relationship to be one of mutual respect and friendship. She was an important figure in my professional life during that period.

Dr. Sturge was a dedicated and inspiring church leader who provided moral support and guidance to me and others in our faith community. He was deeply respected for his devotion and eloquence, especially during Bible sessions such as his review of Corinthians 13. His sudden death left a great void in both my heart and the church.

In 1941, following the outbreak of war in the Pacific, my family and I were first taken to the Tanforan Assembly Center as part of the forced relocation of Japanese Americans. This was a painful and shameful time, as we were separated from our friends and community. The experience marked the beginning of a difficult chapter in my life during the Japanese American internment.

After Tanforan, we were relocated to the Topaz War Relocation Center in the desert of Utah. The harsh desert climate was a stark contrast to the mild environment of San Francisco, and adjusting was very challenging. I taught sewing classes there while my husband worked in the camp infirmary, and we found moments of beauty like watching the brilliant sunsets together.

Utah was where the Topaz War Relocation Center was located, where my family was sent during World War II. The desert environment was difficult to endure, especially coming from the coast of San Francisco. Life there was a test of endurance, but I found ways to contribute by teaching sewing classes and supporting the community.

San Francisco was my home before the war and where my husband and I returned after being released from the internment camps in 1945. Upon return, we found our homes occupied and properties lost, forcing us to share living quarters with other displaced families. Despite these hardships, we rebuilt our lives, with my husband reopening his practice and us renting a house within ten months.
The transcript does not provide specific details about Ransohoff's, so I cannot share a personal story or relationship with this organization based on the information given.
The transcript does not mention Mrs. Mateno, so I have no personal experiences or stories to share about her.

The Japanese American internment during World War II was a deeply painful period in my life. In 1941, my family and I were forcibly removed from our home and sent first to Tanforan Assembly Center and then to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. Despite the injustice and hardship, I maintained my faith and found ways to support others, such as teaching sewing classes. Returning to San Francisco after the war, we faced the loss of our home and property but worked hard to rebuild our lives.
